common_name: Moringa (Drumstick Tree, Miracle Tree, Horseradish Tree)

planting.note: Direct sow seeds (pointed end down, 1 inch deep) or set stem cuttings directly in the ground after last frost in full sun in well-drained, sandy to loamy, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; moringa grows extremely rapidly in warm conditions and should begin producing harvestable leaves within 2–3 months of planting.

directions: Direct sow or plant stem cuttings in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun after all frost danger passes; moringa is extremely drought-tolerant once established and actually performs better in poor, dry soil than in rich, wet conditions. Moringa oleifera (moringa, drumstick tree, or miracle tree) is a North Indian native now cultivated throughout tropical and subtropical regions worldwide as one of the most nutritionally dense multipurpose food trees known — virtually all parts are edible and extraordinarily nutritious: the young leaves are exceptionally high in vitamins A, C, B6, calcium, iron, potassium, and protein and are eaten steamed, added to soups and curries, or dried and powdered as a nutritional supplement; the immature green seed pods (drumsticks) are used in curries; the mature seeds are pressed for edible, high-quality oil (moringa or ben oil); and the roots have a flavor similar to horseradish and are used as a condiment. In zone 9, moringa grows as a fast-growing perennial that may die back to the roots in hard freezes but resprouts vigorously in spring; in zone 10–12, it is a permanent tree. Cut back to 4–5 feet annually to maintain productive, harvestable leaf growth rather than letting it become a large tree.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Moringa
Trees for Life International — Moringa oleifera

planting.note: Direct sow (nick or soak seeds 24 hours) or start transplants indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost; in zone 9, sow in March–April for summer harvest, or again in August for fall harvest; bitter melon requires a trellis or fence to climb and produces prolifically in Gulf Coast heat and humidity.

directions: Nick seeds and sow 1 inch deep in warm, well-drained, fertile, moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun on a trellis or fence; keep soil consistently moist. Momordica charantia (bitter melon, bitter gourd, or karela) is one of the most widely consumed vegetables across South and Southeast Asia, East Africa, and the Caribbean — a fast-growing tropical vine producing distinctive, warty, oblong to tapered green fruits 3–10 inches long that contain a bold, intensely bitter flavor considered an acquired taste but essential in dozens of traditional Asian dishes: Indian karela sabzi (stir-fried bitter melon with onions and spices), Chinese stir-fries with black bean sauce, Filipino ginisang ampalaya, Japanese goya champuru (the classic Okinawan dish). The bitterness decreases substantially when seeds are scooped out and the flesh is salted and rinsed; parboiling further reduces bitterness. Bitter melon has significant traditional medicinal use for blood sugar regulation and is the subject of ongoing pharmaceutical research for antidiabetic compounds. In zone 9, it thrives in summer heat and produces abundantly from June through October; harvest fruits when fully green and before yellowing begins.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Asian Vegetables
University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Bitter Melon

common_name: Smooth Luffa (Loofah, Sponge Gourd, Vegetable Sponge)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 2–4 weeks before last frost; in zone 9, start in February–March for April transplanting; luffa requires a very long, warm growing season (90–120 days of warm weather) to produce mature fibrous sponges — in zone 9, this is easily achieved, and it is one of the best climates in the continental US for productive luffa growing.

directions: Start seeds indoors in peat pots 2–4 weeks before last frost; transplant to full sun in rich, well-drained, deeply cultivated, moist, fertile soil on a very sturdy trellis or fence — luffa vines become heavy and must have strong support. Luffa aegyptiaca (smooth luffa or vegetable sponge) produces dual crops: the immature fruits (3–5 inches long) are eaten as a vegetable throughout Asia and Africa — tasting like a mild, slightly sweet zucchini — while the fully mature, dried fruits are harvested for their fibrous interior skeleton, which dries into the familiar luffa or loofah bath sponge. The vine produces large, bright-yellow flowers (4–5 inches, excellent for pollinators) and cylindrical fruits 18–24 inches long that must be allowed to ripen fully on the vine until the skin yellows and shrivels before harvesting for sponges. To process sponges: cure dried gourds in the sun, peel off the papery skin, shake out seeds (save for next year), wash, and bleach lightly if desired. Zone 9 provides the long, hot season luffa requires to complete its full lifecycle and produce dry, mature sponges.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Vegetables

planting.note: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil reaches 65°F in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; mung bean grows rapidly in warm conditions and is ready for harvest in 55–80 days — multiple successions can be planted from spring through early fall in zone 9 for continuous production.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; inoculate with Rhizobium legume inoculant for best nitrogen fixation. Vigna radiata (mung bean or green gram) is among the most widely consumed legumes in the world — a staple of Indian, Chinese, Southeast Asian, and Korean cuisines in its many forms: whole cooked beans in dal and soups, split (moong dal) for creamy lentil dishes, sprouted (bean sprouts) as a fresh vegetable, ground into flour for cheela pancakes, and processed into bean thread (glass) noodles. The bean is particularly nutritious — high in protein, folate, magnesium, and iron — and easier to digest than many other legumes, producing less flatulence. Mung beans are also the most common source of fresh bean sprouts: seeds germinate and develop edible sprouts in just 3–5 days at room temperature, making them one of the few vegetables that can be produced entirely indoors year-round. In the garden, mung bean grows as a fast-maturing, upright, bushy annual producing small, cylindrical black or brown pods with 8–15 seeds each, harvested when pods turn black-brown and dry.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Legumes

common_name: Winter Melon (Wax Gourd, Ash Gourd, White Gourd)

planting.note: Direct sow or start transplants 3–4 weeks before setting out; in zone 9, sow in March–April for summer-fall harvest; winter melon is one of the most important and widely grown gourds in Chinese and Southeast Asian cuisine and thrives in Gulf Coast heat and humidity.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in well-drained, deeply cultivated, fertile, moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun on a very sturdy trellis or along the ground; keep evenly moist. Benincasa hispida (winter melon or wax gourd) produces the largest cultivated gourd after pumpkins and bottle gourds — round to oblong fruits 8–32 inches long weighing 10–50 pounds, with a white, waxy powder coating the mature skin (which gives it the 'wax gourd' name) and very mild, white, watery flesh that absorbs surrounding flavors in cooking. Winter melon is a cornerstone ingredient in Chinese cuisine — the thick rind is scooped out and the hollowed gourd is used as a serving vessel for delicate winter melon soup garnished with carved decorations; the flesh is added to stir-fries, soups, and dim sum. It is called 'winter melon' despite being a summer crop because the mature fruits store exceptionally well at room temperature for months through winter (up to a year if undamaged), providing a critical source of fresh vegetable during historically difficult winters in Asia. In zone 9, it produces very well in summer heat; the large fruits are harvested in fall when the waxy bloom has fully developed.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Asian Vegetables
Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences — Traditional Vegetables
